Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place. I'm 29, working to support myself with limited resources, so I had to squeeze studying into whatever gaps I could find. I'd usually try to study late at night after everything else was done, or early mornings on weekends. With 18 online courses, I was constantly juggling, and I'd often just focus on whatever was most urgent that day. I tried to set aside about 16 hours a week, but it rarely felt like enough.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on deadlines and what was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd be consistent, logging in every other day, but then something would come up—extra shifts at work, family obligations—and I'd go days without touching the coursework. Deadlines definitely drove my schedule more than any planned routine. I wanted to be more regular, but life just didn't cooperate most of the time.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes I'd get that anxious feeling that I was falling behind, and that would push me to log in and at least see what I was missing. The gamified tasks actually helped—getting those little achievement badges gave me a small reason to check in more often. But honestly, fear of failing was probably my biggest motivator to log in.
Q4: There was a brief period mid-semester when I got into a rhythm with the gamified tasks. Seeing my progress on the leaderboard and earning points made it feel less like a chore and more like something I could actually accomplish. For maybe two or three weeks, I was logging in almost daily. But then my anxiety spiked with some personal stuff, and that routine fell apart. It's hard to maintain habits when you're constantly overwhelmed.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely useful—I watched nearly 300 minutes of them because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the material at my own pace. The discussion posts also helped me feel connected to other students, which mattered since I often felt isolated studying alone. The gamified elements surprised me by keeping me engaged longer than I expected. Reading materials I could download were great too because I could study them offline when I didn't have reliable internet.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the optional readings and supplementary materials because I simply didn't have time. With 18 courses and limited hours, I had to prioritize ruthlessly. Some of the peer interaction features I used minimally because my anxiety made it hard to put myself out there. I also delayed discussion posts until the last minute because writing them felt overwhelming when I was already stressed about assignments.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to passing the course or helped me understand something I was struggling with. The gamified tasks were clever because they made even small activities feel rewarding with points and badges. If I could see immediate progress or get feedback, that motivated me. But if something felt like busywork with no clear payoff, I'd skip it—I just couldn't afford to waste time.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones I'd force myself to do even when exhausted, but optional ones got pushed aside constantly. There was always this guilt about not doing them, knowing they might help, but the pressure of required work always won. The gamified optional tasks were different though—they tricked my brain into wanting to complete them for the rewards, even when I was tired.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded PDFs and lecture slides to study on my phone during bus rides or breaks at work. I'd take handwritten notes in a notebook because writing helps me remember things better than just reading on screen. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos or free resources online that explained concepts differently. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS entirely.
Q10: There were many nights I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or re-reading downloaded materials without ever logging in. I'd quiz myself using flashcards I made, or I'd discuss concepts with a friend who was taking similar courses. The LMS wouldn't show any of that effort. Some of my best studying happened on paper, completely invisible to the system.
Q11: Yes, frequently. I'd download a lecture video or PDF, watch or read it once online, then continue studying it offline for days. My internet access isn't always reliable, so offline studying was essential. The LMS would only show that initial download or view, not the hours I spent afterward reviewing the same material repeatedly until I understood it.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially when my anxiety was peaking. Around midterms, I felt completely overwhelmed—18 courses, work, and personal responsibilities all colliding. I'd look at the LMS and feel paralyzed, not knowing where to start. My resilience isn't great, so setbacks hit me hard. During those times, even logging in felt like too much.
Q13: Constantly. When I was exhausted from work, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Confusion about course material made me avoid certain modules entirely because I didn't know how to approach them. Competing responsibilities meant I'd often choose sleep or work over studying. The LMS became something I dreaded opening during my most overwhelmed periods.
Q14: Many times. I'd have the course page open, scrolling through materials, but my mind would be elsewhere—worrying about bills, thinking about work, or just too tired to focus. I'd watch videos but realize at the end I hadn't retained anything. Those sessions looked like engagement in the data, but they were really just me going through the motions while mentally checked out.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. My anxiety made starting things incredibly difficult—I'd worry about doing them wrong, so I'd avoid them entirely. Sometimes I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I was less tired, but tomorrow never came. The irony is that avoiding them only increased my anxiety, creating this terrible cycle I couldn't break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd have a burst of activity right before something was due, cramming everything into a few desperate hours. My LMS usage probably looked like nothing for days, then sudden intense activity. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only way I could function with everything else going on. The deadline pressure was both motivating and terrifying.
Q17: When I got positive feedback, it genuinely boosted my mood and made me want to engage more. But negative or critical feedback sometimes made me avoid that course for a while because of my anxiety. The gamified feedback—points, badges, progress bars—was easier to handle emotionally than written critiques. Quick feedback helped me stay on track; delayed feedback made me feel disconnected from my own work.
Q18: The course structure was overwhelming with 18 courses running simultaneously. When modules were clearly organized with obvious priorities, I could manage better. But when everything seemed equally important, I'd freeze up. The gamified elements helped by giving me smaller, achievable goals within the larger chaos. Courses with flexible deadlines were easier on my anxiety than rigid ones.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier—they broke things into manageable pieces and gave me immediate rewards. But the sheer volume of content across all my courses made it harder. Some courses had confusing navigation that wasted my limited time. Clear, simple layouts with obvious next steps helped enormously. Courses that felt like they understood students have limited time were much easier to engage with.
Q20: I think that describes me sometimes. I'd be clicking around, watching videos, participating in discussions—all visible activity—but struggling to actually complete and submit assignments. The anxiety of submitting imperfect work stopped me, even when I'd done the learning. Sometimes understanding the material and proving you understand it are two different challenges. High activity can mean someone is trying hard but hitting invisible barriers.
Q21: That makes sense too. Someone might download everything once, study intensively offline, and perform well on exams without much LMS presence. I did some of that—my exam score was decent even though my assignment completion was low. Real learning doesn't always happen on platforms. Some people are just efficient or prefer different study methods that don't require constant logging in.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend. But they miss the quality of that time entirely. They can't see if you're actually understanding or just scrolling. They miss offline studying, emotional struggles, life circumstances, and the difference between productive engagement and anxious procrastination. Numbers tell a story, but it's never the whole story.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the material, connecting it to things I know, and caring about understanding it. It's not just logging in or watching videos—it's that mental investment where you're genuinely curious or determined to learn. For me, engagement also means feeling like I can succeed, not just going through motions while drowning in anxiety. True engagement requires both presence and hope.
Q24: The LMS can't show my anxiety levels, how hard I'm fighting just to keep going, or the hours I spend studying offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. It can't capture the nights I stayed up worrying about falling behind or the genuine effort behind my low completion rates. It doesn't show that I'm 29, working with limited resources, trying to change my life through education despite everything working against me. The struggle and determination are invisible in the data.
